researchFindings.claim: The universal distress signal is three of anything in succession — three whistle blasts, three mirror flashes, three fires in a triangle or straight line
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Signaling for help in the wilderness

researchFindings.claim: A high-decibel emergency whistle (120 dB) can be heard up to one mile in forested areas; blow three short sharp blasts, wait 30–60 seconds, then repeat
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Signaling for help in the wilderness

researchFindings.claim: A properly aimed signal mirror can be seen up to 10 miles away on a clear day; aim by sighting the target (aircraft, person) between two outstretched V-fingers while reflecting sunlight through them
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Mirror signaling step with aiming technique

researchFindings.claim: If you don't have a signal mirror, any reflective surface works — belt buckle, polished metal container, space blanket, or smartphone screen
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Signaling for help in the wilderness

researchFindings.claim: Ground-to-air signals (X, SOS, or V) made from rocks, logs, or dug trenches should have each line at least 10 feet wide to be visible from aircraft; a 5:1 length-to-width ratio is recommended
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Signaling for help in the wilderness

researchFindings.claim: Choose an open, visible location for signaling — a clearing, ridgetop, riverbank, or open rock — to maximize line-of-sight for both visual and auditory signals

Stay put once you begin signaling — search and rescue teams begin from the last known location and follow the planned route; moving away from your last known point dramatically enlarges the search area
Personal Locator Beacons (PLBs) registered with NOAA transmit GPS coordinates to monitored satellite systems; when activated they take the 'search' out of search and rescue
Signal fires should only be lit when you hear or see an aircraft or ground searchers — add green vegetation (pine boughs, green leaves) to create dense smoke visible from the air in daylight
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Signaling for help in the wilderness
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Fire/smoke signaling step and safety note

researchFindings.claim: Balance signaling with survival fundamentals — shelter, water, and warmth — so you remain physically capable of signaling when rescuers arrive
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Balance principle — prevents over-exertion on signaling

conflictsOrWeakClaims.issue: Sources cite 10 miles to 50 miles range for signal mirrors. The 10-mile figure is conservative and well-attested; 50 miles appears in some sources but is exceptional and depends on altitude and atmospheric conditions.
conflictsOrWeakClaims.resolution: Use '10 miles on a clear day' as the conservative, more reliable figure

identifiedGaps.area: Authoritative government source specifically on wilderness rescue signaling
identifiedGaps.impact: This topic is more thoroughly covered by established outdoor education organizations (BSA, Canadian SAR) than by government agencies. NOAA covers PLBs but not physical signaling techniques. The evidence tier authoritative_plus_field_practice is appropriate.
identifiedGaps.suggestion: Set sourceQuality to 'mixed' — content is from reputable field-practice sources but not primarily government agencies. This is appropriate for the topic.

recommendation: Upgrade signal-for-rescue action_card. Replace the existing NIST/DOT sources which are wholly inappropriate (NIST telecom and DOT rail transit are irrelevant to wilderness rescue signaling). New content: (1) stop and choose visible location, (2) rule of three — whistle, mirror, fire, (3) whistle: three blasts, pause, repeat, (4) signal mirror technique with V-finger aiming, (5) ground-to-air X/SOS, (6) fire signals only on searcher contact, (7) stay put, (8) balance with survival basics. Differentiate summary from quick_answer. Set sourceQuality: mixed (BSA/AdventureSmart are established but not government). This is a significant improvement over the current guide which has NIST telecom sources.
